subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree in Urban Planning from a state university
Completed coursework in GIS, zoning law, and municipal policy during undergraduate studies
Attended several professional development workshops on land use and permitting processes
Participated in a capstone project focused on revitalizing small-town downtown districts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Currently holds the title of Senior Urban and Regional Planner
Works for a government department serving smaller municipal entities
Acts as a primary contact for zoning and permitting questions for part-time city officials
Frequently reviews zoning changes and building permit applications for compliance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the public sector, specifically local government planning
Industry focus is urban and regional planning, zoning, and permitting
Supports municipalities with limited in-house planning resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed by a mid-sized regional government department with approximately 50 staff
Works in a collaborative, office-based environment with hybrid remote options
Team includes planners, GIS specialists, and administrative staff
Supports a network of dozens of small towns and rural communities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Serves the public sector, focusing on municipal and regional planning
Primary clients are smaller towns and rural entities lacking full-time planners
Work involves both residential and commercial development review

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 6-10 years of experience in the urban planning field
Spent the majority of professional career within the same department
Progressed from junior planner to senior planner over the past decade

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior-level position, making independent decisions on complex cases
Often mentors newer planning staff and interns
Trusted to represent the department in meetings with municipal officials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Reviews and approves zoning and permitting applications for compliance
Advises small municipalities on zoning law and permitting procedures
Conducts phone calls and meetings with local officials to resolve planning issues
Uses GIS software to analyze land use and generate maps for reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time on plan and permit review
Allocates around 30% to meetings and phone consultations with local officials
Uses about 20% of time for email correspondence and follow-ups
Devotes the remaining 10% to research, reporting, and tax incentive analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has significant autonomy in interpreting and applying zoning codes
Authorized to approve or request revisions for most standard permit applications
Seeks guidance from department leadership only on highly unusual or controversial cases
Empowered to initiate meetings with local officials to resolve complex issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly assesses routine zoning permit applications for residential builds
Comfortably uses GIS software to create and annotate basic land-use maps
Efficiently responds to common zoning inquiries via email or phone

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Navigating complex zoning variances or appeals that involve multiple stakeholders
Researching and applying new or ambiguous state-level development regulations
Resolving disputes between developers and local residents over proposed projects
Coordinating multi-entity meetings to address inter-jurisdictional planning issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Extracting and cross-referencing zoning codes for permit applications
Compiling standard data tables and summary reports on permit approvals
Responding to frequently asked questions about zoning rules
Scheduling routine meetings with municipal contacts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys collaborating with local officials on strategic community planning
Finds interpreting complex zoning issues intellectually stimulating
Considers repetitive data entry and checking for permit completeness tedious
Prefers in-person or interactive meetings over lengthy email chains

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Experiences delays due to inconsistent data formats from different municipalities
Manual data entry for permit tracking is time-consuming and error-prone
Lack of standardized forms and workflows across entities creates confusion
Difficulty retrieving historical permit data for reference

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Highest productivity occurs when working on tasks that require both expertise and creative problem-solving
Enjoys tasks more when able to use advanced GIS features to visualize solutions
Skill level in zoning law correlates with confidence and efficiency in application review
Tedious, low-skill tasks tend to negatively impact both enjoyment and output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Views adoption of new technology as essential for efficiency
Embraces opportunities to test out improved planning software
Enjoys exploring new AI features that can automate routine tasks
Prefers hands-on learning to theoretical instruction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to updates in GIS and permitting software
Regularly seeks out online resources or tutorials for new tools
Occasionally assists colleagues in troubleshooting software problems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to formal or self-guided tech training
Participated in recent AI and automation webinars offered by the department
Believes tech training is a good investment for reducing long-term workload

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential for reducing repetitive tasks and streamlining workflows
Occasional barrier: heavy workload can make it challenging to prioritize training
Prefers new tech that directly relates to core planning responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tips about new GIS plugins or AI tools, encouraging experimentation
Management supports tech adoption by providing training resources
Team culture values sharing knowledge and best practices for tool use

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses ESRI ArcGIS daily for mapping and spatial analysis
Relies on proprietary permitting management software tailored for municipal entities
Regularly utilizes Microsoft Excel for data analysis and reporting
Drafts correspondence and reports in Microsoft Word

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses Outlook for email communication and scheduling
Refers to state and municipal zoning code databases
Participates in phone and video calls via Microsoft Teams

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in GIS platforms including ArcGIS and QGIS
Experienced in using standard permitting and workflow management software
Familiar with document version control and digital signature tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces data export/import issues between GIS and permitting software
Manual re-entry of permit data required when systems lack integration
Discrepancies arise when municipalities use outdated or non-standard file formats
Would benefit from better integration between email/calendar and workflow management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands concepts such as natural language processing and machine learning
Comfortable with terminology like LLM (Large Language Model), prompt, and dataset
Familiar with the basics of AI-powered document summarization and chatbots
Keeps up-to-date with AI innovation via webinars and professional groups

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses OpenAI's ChatGPT daily for research and drafting responses
Employs Microsoft Copilot within Office 365 for document and email drafting
Tests proprietary AI-powered permit pre-screening tool piloted by the department

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily to draft responses to zoning inquiries and summarize regulations
Applies AI tools to extract and cross-reference relevant code sections for permits
Employs AI assistants for scheduling meetings and generating standard reports
Leverages AI to analyze public feedback on planning proposals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-based document summarization saved hours on reviewing lengthy commission meeting minutes
Experienced occasional errors when AI misinterpreted legal zoning language, requiring manual correction
Found AI chatbots helpful for answering basic zoning FAQs, but struggled with nuanced cases
Learned to always verify AI-generated reports before submission

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Department offers quarterly training sessions on new AI tools
Peers frequently share custom AI prompts and workflows via internal forums
Access to help documents and video tutorials for AI applications is readily available
Leadership encourages ongoing learning and experimentation with AI

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex or unfamiliar tasks
Uses AI independently for routine document drafting or information retrieval
Finds iterative feedback with AI helpful for refining zoning memos

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for administrative and research tasks
Occasionally leverages AI as an advisor when brainstorming solutions to new zoning issues
Relies on AI as a co-worker when handling large volumes of permit applications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Very open to adopting AI-driven workflows when they improve efficiency
Advocates for piloting AI tools before full-scale implementation
Willing to help design and test new AI workflow integrations for planning processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for exploring zoning queries with LLMs
Finds command-based interfaces more efficient for batch data processing
Believes a mixed approach (conversational for brainstorming, command-based for tasks) is ideal

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many zoning decisions require deep understanding of local codes and precedents
Tacit knowledge about local politics and stakeholder preferences often guides recommendations
AI can assist with research but final decisions require human judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for gathering and summarizing information, but not for final permit approvals
Always manually reviews AI outputs for accuracy before acting on them
Confidence in AI increases when outputs are transparent and explainable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive data analysis and initial document drafting
Believes human oversight is necessary for interpreting zoning law or handling exceptions
Envisions a workflow where AI handles 60% of routine tasks and humans focus on higher-level decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for standardized, low-risk permitting processes
Feels automation is threatening when it risks overlooking local context or stakeholder input
Supports manual override and review mechanisms for all AI-driven decisions

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and critical thinking skills
Sees reduced need for manual data entry and routine documentation skills
Views domain expertise as increasingly important for interpreting AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developed skill in prompt engineering for effective LLM use
Increasingly responsible for validating and auditing AI-generated content
Learning to design workflows that combine AI automation with human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern about automation of basic permitting, but confident in continued need for expert review
Believes nuanced tasks and stakeholder engagement will remain human-driven
Open to role evolving toward more strategic and oversight functions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated permit recommendations require human approval before issuance
Department maintains clear protocols for manual review of AI outputs
Regular spot-checks and audits are conducted on AI-assisted workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Department has formalized guidelines for responsible AI use in planning
Recent policy updates mandate transparency and accountability in AI-assisted decisions
Team is encouraged to provide feedback on AI tool performance for continuous improvement

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as having potential to level the playing field for under-resourced communities
Believes AI will significantly streamline government operations and service delivery
Cautiously optimistic about the impact of AI on job quality and public engagement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes AI must be transparent, accountable, and free from bias in public sector use
Concerned about overreliance on AI for decisions that affect community well-being
Supports ongoing ethical review and public input on AI deployment in planning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from an AI-powered zoning code navigator with real-time Q&A
Desires an AI assistant that can auto-generate meeting summaries from recordings
Sees value in an AI tool for automated cross-municipality permit status tracking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine permitting and code lookup tasks to be mostly automated
Foresees a shift toward more community engagement and strategic planning work
Anticipates greater oversight responsibility for AI-driven workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Wants organization to invest in continuous AI training and upskilling for staff
Recommends establishment of an AI ethics committee for planning applications
Advocates for pilot programs to test AI solutions before broad deployment
Supports creating feedback loops to refine AI tools based on user experience
